map.condition: Negative weights or detect negative cycles, single-source

formulas.name: Dijkstra's Time Complexity (Naive / Array)
formulas.whenToUse: When Dijkstra is implemented with a simple array (linear scan) for the priority queue
formulas.trap: This is the complexity for dense graphs or when a simple array is used — do NOT quote O(V^2) when a binary heap is specified.

formulas.name: Dijkstra's Time Complexity (Binary Heap)
formulas.whenToUse: When Dijkstra is implemented using a binary min-heap (standard efficient implementation)
formulas.trap: Often simplified to O(E log V) for connected graphs since E >= V-1. Do not confuse with O(E log E).

formulas.name: Dijkstra's Time Complexity (Fibonacci Heap)
formulas.whenToUse: When Dijkstra is implemented with a Fibonacci heap (theoretically optimal)
formulas.trap: Fibonacci heap gives better asymptotic complexity but high constant factors; rarely used in practice. GATE may ask which heap achieves O(E + V log V).

formulas.name: Dijkstra Correctness Condition
formulas.plain: Dijkstra is correct if and only if all edge weights >= 0
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether Dijkstra can be applied to a given graph
formulas.trap: Dijkstra FAILS with negative edge weights — it may not relax edges correctly once a vertex is finalized. Use Bellman-Ford instead.

formulas.name: Dijkstra on Complete Graph
formulas.plain: On complete graph E = Theta(V^2), so heap-based Dijkstra is O(V^2 log V); array-based is O(V^2)
formulas.whenToUse: When the graph is complete or dense and you need Dijkstra's complexity

terms.means: number of edges; E = V(V-1)/2 for complete undirected

formulas.trap: For complete graphs, simple array-based Dijkstra O(V^2) is better than heap-based O(V^2 log V).

formulas.name: Bellman-Ford Time Complexity
formulas.whenToUse: Any single-source shortest path problem where negative weights may exist
formulas.trap: Bellman-Ford runs V-1 iterations of edge relaxation. On a complete graph E = V(V-1), so complexity becomes O(V^3).

formulas.name: Bellman-Ford on Complete Graph
formulas.plain: On complete graph E = Theta(V^2), Bellman-Ford is O(V^3)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked Bellman-Ford complexity on a complete graph of n vertices

terms.means: number of edges; V(V-1) for complete directed graph

formulas.trap: A complete directed graph on n vertices has n(n-1) edges, giving O(n * n^2) = O(n^3). Option Theta(n^2) is wrong.

formulas.name: Bellman-Ford Edge Relaxation (V-1 passes)
formulas.latex: \text{Repeat } (V-1) \text{ times: } d[v] = \min(d[v],\, d[u] + w(u,v)) \text{ for all edges } (u,v)
formulas.plain: Repeat (V-1) times: d[v] = min(d[v], d[u] + w(u,v)) for all edges (u,v)
formulas.whenToUse: Understanding the core relaxation loop of Bellman-Ford

terms.means: current shortest distance estimate to vertex v
weight of edge from u to v
maximum number of edges on any simple shortest path

formulas.trap: After V-1 passes, if any distance can still be reduced, a negative-weight cycle is reachable from the source.

formulas.name: Bellman-Ford Negative-Cycle Detection
formulas.latex: \text{If } d[v] > d[u] + w(u,v) \text{ after } (V-1) \text{ passes} \Rightarrow \text{negative cycle reachable from source}
formulas.plain: If after (V-1) passes any edge (u,v) still satisfies d[v] > d[u] + w(u,v), a negative cycle is reachable from source
formulas.whenToUse: When asked what Bellman-Ford reports about negative cycles
formulas.trap: Bellman-Ford detects only negative cycles REACHABLE from the source. It does NOT find all negative cycles in the graph.

formulas.name: Floyd-Warshall Time Complexity
formulas.whenToUse: All-pairs shortest path on a dense graph; recurrence iterates over all intermediate vertices
formulas.trap: Floyd-Warshall handles negative edges but NOT negative cycles. It gives incorrect results if a negative cycle exists.

formulas.name: Floyd-Warshall Recurrence
formulas.whenToUse: Fill-in-the-blank or code-completion questions about Floyd-Warshall template

terms.means: shortest path from i to j using only vertices 1..k as intermediates

formulas.trap: The outer loop must be over k (intermediate vertex), not i or j. Getting loop order wrong is a classic GATE trap.

formulas.name: BFS for Unweighted Shortest Path
formulas.whenToUse: Finding shortest path (fewest edges) in an unweighted or 0-1 weight graph
formulas.trap: BFS gives shortest paths only for unweighted (or unit-weight) graphs. For weighted graphs, BFS distance is NOT the shortest path.

formulas.name: DAG Shortest Path via Topological Sort
formulas.latex: O(V + E) \text{ — topological sort then single-pass edge relaxation}
formulas.plain: O(V + E) — topological sort then single-pass edge relaxation
formulas.whenToUse: Shortest (or longest) path in a DAG, even with negative edge weights
formulas.trap: DAG shortest path works with negative edges (no cycles so no negative cycle possible). Much faster than Bellman-Ford for DAGs.

formulas.name: 0-1 BFS (Deque) for 0/1 Weighted Graph
formulas.latex: O(V + E) \text{ using deque; 0-weight edges push front, 1-weight edges push back}
formulas.plain: O(V + E) using deque; push front for 0-weight edges, push back for 1-weight edges
formulas.whenToUse: Shortest path when edge weights are only 0 or 1 (e.g. subgraph E1 weight 0, rest weight 1)
formulas.trap: Do not use Dijkstra (O(E log V)) when BFS or 0-1 BFS suffices in O(V+E) — the question may test choosing the most efficient algorithm.

formulas.name: Shortest Path Algorithm Summary
formulas.latex: \begin{array}{ll} \text{BFS (unweighted)} & O(V+E) \\ \text{DAG (topo + relax)} & O(V+E) \\ \text{Dijkstra (array)} & O(V^2) \\ \text{Dijkstra (bin heap)} & O((V+E)\log V) \\ \text{Dijkstra (Fib heap)} & O(E + V\log V) \\ \text{Bellman-Ford} & O(VE) \\ \text{Floyd-Warshall} & O(V^3) \end{array}
formulas.plain: BFS: O(V+E) | DAG: O(V+E) | Dijkstra array: O(V^2) | Dijkstra heap: O((V+E)logV) | Dijkstra Fib: O(E+VlogV) | Bellman-Ford: O(VE) | Floyd-Warshall: O(V^3)
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing algorithms to pick the most efficient for given constraints
formulas.trap: Warshall's algorithm (transitive closure) is NOT the same as Floyd-Warshall shortest path — they are different algorithms despite similar structure.

formulas.name: All-Pairs via Repeated Single-Source
formulas.plain: Repeat Dijkstra V times => O(V*(V+E)*log V)
formulas.whenToUse: When comparing Floyd-Warshall O(V^3) vs repeated Dijkstra for all-pairs shortest path
formulas.trap: For sparse graphs, repeated Dijkstra is faster than Floyd-Warshall; for dense graphs, Floyd-Warshall (O(V^3)) may be comparable or preferred.

formulas.name: Algorithm Capability with Negative Weights
formulas.latex: \text{Dijkstra: NO} \quad \text{Bellman-Ford: YES (detects neg cycle)} \quad \text{Floyd-Warshall: YES (no neg cycle)}
formulas.plain: Dijkstra: cannot handle negative weights. Bellman-Ford: YES, detects negative cycles. Floyd-Warshall: YES, but incorrect if negative cycle exists.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked which algorithm handles negative edge weights correctly
formulas.trap: Bellman-Ford handles negative edge weights and DETECTS negative cycles; Floyd-Warshall handles negative edges but FAILS if a negative cycle exists.
